title: Supporting the designation of March 15, 2025, as "National Osceola Turkey Day".
plain_summary: This bill asks Congress to make March 15, 2025 a special day called National Osceola Turkey Day. The day would honor a type of wild turkey that only lives in Florida. The bill says that turkey hunting helps Florida's economy and helps protect these birds. It wants all Americans to celebrate this special day to learn about these unique Florida turkeys.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution supports making March 15, 2025, a day called National Osceola Turkey Day.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: Wild turkeys have been important to American history and family traditions. Wild turkey was served at the first Thanksgiving dinner, and turkey is still eaten during many holidays today. Wild turkey is a healthy source of lean protein that is organic and tastes good. Turkey hunters in the United States spend about seventy-six million nine hundred thousand dollars each year since 1985, which creates an economic impact of one hundred twenty-eight million seven hundred thousand dollars every year. Florida has a long history of wild turkey hunting, managing turkey populations, and studying turkeys. Florida runs the Wild Turkey Cost Share Program, which is the biggest partnership between government and private groups in the United States for keeping wild turkey habitat healthy on wildlife management areas and other public lands where hunting is allowed. Since this program started in 1994, more than one million acres of upland habitat have received money for turkey habitat management work. During Florida's 2024 spring wild turkey hunting season, an estimated thirty-one thousand eighty-five hunters took part in turkey hunting, including about five thousand four hundred fifty-three people who live outside of Florida. In Florida, money made from selling wild turkey permits pays for conservation work, research, and management of wild turkeys, and for promoting the cultural heritage of hunting. Turkey hunters are an important part of the Wild Turkey Cost Share Program. The money from selling turkey permits, which hunters must buy to hunt wild turkeys in Florida unless they are exempt, lets the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission give significant money to the Wild Turkey Cost Share Program each year. Florida is home to two types of wild turkey: the eastern wild turkey and the Osceola or Florida wild turkey. The Osceola is one of five types of wild turkey in North America. The Osceola turkey lives only in peninsular Florida. The Osceola type of wild turkey is often seen as mysterious and the hardest to hunt because it lives in a small area and is often found in swampy places. Hunters who want to catch all four types of turkey in the United States, called a Grand Slam, must hunt in Florida. March 1, 2025, is the opening day of turkey hunting season in part of Florida, and March 15, 2025, is the opening day for the entire state of Florida.

sections.title: What the House Decides
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports making March 15, 2025, National Osceola Turkey Day. The House encourages all Americans to celebrate National Osceola Turkey Day with proper ceremonies and activities.
